headline_of_the_day: Judge blocks Paramount-Warner Bros merger on antitrust grounds

key_points.text: A judge paused the Paramount-Warner Bros merger for 14 days while states argue it will raise prices and cut shows.
Hackers are exploiting two serious WordPress flaws from last week, putting tens of millions of websites at risk.
Craneware, which makes hospital software, said hackers stole data from thousands of US hospitals and pharmacies.
The EU fined AliExpress 550 million euros for selling unsafe toys and fake products on its site.

stories.title: LG monitors install McAfee ads on Windows PCs without user permission
stories.summary: Windows Update quietly installs an LG driver and app when a new LG UltraGear monitor plugs into your PC.

article.background: LG makes UltraGear monitors. These are high-end displays aimed at gamers and professionals. When you plug one into a Windows 11 PC for the first time, the system automatically installs software updates.
article.what_happened: Windows Update installs LG's driver updates and an app called LG Monitor App Installer without asking permission. The app does not have its own controls. Instead it shows you links to LG's other apps and McAfee antivirus. The app also sends unwanted ad notifications to your screen.
article.why_it_matters: If you own an LG UltraGear monitor on Windows 11, ads will pop up on your screen. You have to find and remove the LG Monitor App Installer yourself to make them stop. The software installs without your consent or knowledge.

stories.title: Complete technical guide explains how New York subway signals work
stories.summary: A guide explains the signaling system that NYC subway trains use to stay safe and run on time.

article.background: The New York City subway uses a signal system called wayside color-light block signalling. Signals sit beside the tracks rather than overhead. They use colored lights to show whether a train can proceed. The system divides tracks into blocks, or sections. Electrical sensors in each block detect when a train is present.
article.what_happened: Nearly all current subway routes use this same classic signalling method. The core principles have stayed the same for many years. The system works by placing signals at the side of tracks. Colored lights tell trains whether the next block is clear.
article.why_it_matters: The signalling system keeps trains safely spaced apart on the tracks. If you ride the subway, this is how operators know where every train is. Understanding how signals work explains why trains sometimes stop or slow down between stations. The system's age means upgrades are ongoing but the basic method remains reliable.

stories.title: Chinese AI lab Moonshot releases Kimi K3 model with promised open weights
stories.summary: Moonshot will release the code for its new Kimi K3 AI model on July 27, intensifying open AI competition.

article.background: Moonshot AI, a Chinese lab, released Kimi K3 on July 16th. K3 is a large language model, the kind of AI that powers chatbots. The model has 2.8 trillion parameters, which are settings that shape how it responds. Moonshot promised to release K3's internal weights, the blueprints that let others run it, on July 27th. This matters because most advanced AI models are closed. Companies like OpenAI keep their blueprints secret.
article.what_happened: Moonshot said it will release K3's weights publicly. This would make a cutting-edge model available to anyone with enough computing power. The announcement sparked debate about open versus closed AI models. OpenAI's head of strategic futures argued the US should create doubt about open models. Other tech leaders, including Yann LeCun, countered that open models drive faster innovation.
article.why_it_matters: If Moonshot releases the weights as promised, the economics of AI shift. Right now, companies charge money to access their best models. With K3's weights public, anyone could run something equally powerful for free. This could reshape who profits from AI and where the best research happens. The debate over open models is really a fight about whether closed companies or the broader research community should lead AI's future.

stories.title: AI system proves 20-year-old graph theory conjecture with ten-line algorithm
stories.summary: Researchers used AI to solve a math problem about the best way to match items in a data stream.

stories.title: Federal judge blocks Paramount-Warner Bros merger on antitrust grounds
stories.summary: A judge stopped the merger for 14 days after states said the deal would raise prices and cut TV shows.

article.background: Paramount and Warner Bros. Discovery want to merge into one company. The deal is worth about $110 billion. A group of state attorneys general, led by California's Rob Bonta, sued to block it. They say the merger would break antitrust law, the rule that stops companies from merging in ways that hurt competition.
article.what_happened: Federal Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín ordered a 14-day pause on Monday. She ruled after hearing arguments on Friday. The judge said the states raised serious questions about whether the merger would break antitrust law. She also said the states would face harm without the pause. Paramount had already agreed not to close the deal before July 22.
article.why_it_matters: If the merger happens, the combined company would control a huge share of movies and TV shows. The judge said she believes the deal could violate antitrust law and harm competition. That could mean fewer choices and higher prices for viewers. The states can ask for another pause after 14 days if needed.

stories.title: European Union fines AliExpress record 550 million euros for illegal goods
stories.summary: The EU fined AliExpress 550 million euros for failing to stop sales of unsafe toys and counterfeit goods.

article.background: AliExpress is a Chinese online marketplace where sellers list products for sale, similar to eBay or Amazon. The EU has laws requiring platforms to check for illegal or unsafe goods. These include counterfeit clothes and toys that fail safety tests. The Digital Services Act sets strict rules for how platforms must monitor their listings.
article.what_happened: The European Commission fined AliExpress €550 million (£467 million) for breaking these rules. Investigators found the company did not properly assess risks from illegal products. AliExpress failed to build teams dedicated to removing counterfeit and dangerous items. Reviewers were sometimes given only tens of seconds to evaluate flagged products. The company also made it easy for sellers to hide from detection.
article.why_it_matters: If you buy from AliExpress, you may receive unsafe or fake products. The company's weak checks mean dangerous cosmetics, broken toy safety standards, and counterfeit goods can slip through. This fine is the largest yet under EU law. It signals that the EU will hold platforms accountable for what sellers offer on their sites.

stories.title: Hackers exploit two critical WordPress bugs affecting tens of millions of sites
stories.summary: WordPress fixed two serious flaws last week and urged users to update quickly as hackers attack the sites.

article.background: WordPress is free software that millions of websites use to publish content and run their sites. Last week, WordPress released patches for two critical security flaws. These bugs were severe enough that WordPress enabled forced updates where possible.
article.what_happened: Hackers are now breaking into websites running the vulnerable software. Three cybersecurity firms, Patchstack, Hexastrike, and WatchTowr, confirmed the attacks are happening. As of Monday, tens of millions of WordPress websites had not yet installed the patches.
article.why_it_matters: If your website runs WordPress, you need to update it now. Hackers can break into unpatched sites without a password or special access. Site owners who delay risk losing control of their site and exposing visitor data.

stories.title: Healthcare billing software maker Craneware discloses breach affecting hospital data
stories.summary: Craneware said hackers stole data from thousands of US hospitals and pharmacies that use its software.

article.background: Craneware is a U.K.-based company that makes billing and accounting software. Thousands of U.S. hospitals, clinics, and pharmacies use it to manage patient billing and financial records.
article.what_happened: Hackers stole a significant amount of customer data from Craneware's systems. The company announced the breach on Monday. Attackers took employee data, customer data, and partner records. Craneware said the hackers have been removed from its systems. The investigation is still ongoing.
article.why_it_matters: A breach like this could expose patient information. It might also disrupt how hospitals and pharmacies handle payments and records. The company has not said exactly what data was stolen.

stories.title: New malware hides stolen data in Microsoft 365 calendar events dated to 2050
stories.summary: HollowGraph malware hides stolen files in Microsoft 365 calendars to sneak them past security tools.

article.background: Researchers at cybersecurity firm Group-IB discovered a new malware called HollowGraph. It hijacks a Microsoft 365 calendar in a compromised account. The malware hides commands and stolen files as attachments on fake calendar events. Those events are dated to the year 2050.
article.what_happened: HollowGraph uses Microsoft's Graph API to move stolen data and commands through normal Microsoft 365 traffic. This makes the activity look like routine calendar use. The malware is a .NET DLL file that only supports two commands: get and send. It never connects to servers owned by the attackers.
article.why_it_matters: Organizations using Microsoft 365 may have this malware moving data and commands through their systems without triggering alerts. Security tools trust Microsoft's legitimate services by default, so activity hidden inside them is harder to spot. The technique shows attackers can hide inside cloud services that most organizations already rely on.
